We are thrilled to share details about the Asset and Service Management presence at this three-day event in Orlando, Florida, from May 11-13.

Check out the list of can’t-miss Asset and Service Management sessions we’ve compiled below:

Reimagine asset management and field service with AI assistants and agents | SCM1733

Monday, May 11 | 03:00 p.m.

Explore how the latest advancements in AI are redefining the roles of maintenance planners, reliability engineers, schedulers, dispatchers, and field technicians. We explore how AI assistants and agents are reshaping day-to-day operations and helping improve asset uptime and end-user productivity.

Scale as you grow: SAP Cloud ERP for resilient supply chains | ERP2449

Monday, May 11 | 03:30 p.m.

Modernize your supply chain and reduce unplanned disruptions with SAP Cloud ERP public. Real‑time insights and AI agents help you balance demand and supply, automate maintenance, and spot risks early—improving quality, reducing waste, and enabling on‑time, efficient delivery as you scale.

Supercharging field service with AI at Summit Utilities | SCM1787

Tuesday, May 12 | 10:30 a.m.

Learn how Summit Utilities Inc. uses the SAP Field Service Management solution and embedded AI capabilities to improve customer service, maintain critical infrastructure, and support technicians and dispatchers with intelligent workflows.

Next-generation supply chain | SAP2668

Tuesday, May 12 | 10:30 a.m.

Explore how intelligent agents reshape supply chain management by enabling highly adaptive, agentic systems that evolve with constant change. These intelligent capabilities help organizations foresee disruptions, act instantly, and continuously optimize end‑to‑end operations.

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences: Maintenance | SAP2669

Tuesday, May 12 | 11:30 a.m.

Maintenance is entering an AI-driven era. See how agentic AI can be used to detect issues early, execute planning and dispatch, and guide technicians in the field. With the entire workflow turned into an intelligent engine, teams can boost uptime and elevate maintenance performance.

Extend procurement and supply chain collaboration with RISE with SAP | SCM2141

Tuesday, May 12 | 01:30 p.m.

New customers on the RISE with SAP journey can accelerate value by activating their entitlement to SAP Business Network. Learn how connected procurement, supply chain, logistics, and asset management processes enhance collaboration, increase visibility, and drive resilient operations across your enterprise.

Dynamically adjusting supply chains: Orchestrate, adapt, and outperform | KEY2569

Tuesday, May 12 | 02:00 p.m.

CEOs see supply chains as strategic assets—and COOs and chief supply chain officers must deliver. SAP software unites AI, contextual data, and apps to reimagine operations. From a user experience enhanced by the Joule solution to agent orchestration, transform your supply chain into a resilient, high-performance engine.

Industrial AI agents: Connecting the manufacturing value chain | IND2406

Tuesday, May 12 | 02:30 p.m.

Leaders from Kaeser Compressors and Vestas Wind Systems A/S explain how SAP’s industrial AI agents transform manufacturing, elevating domain experts from task executors to informed decision-makers. From faster tender analysis to autonomous planning, AI agents connect engineering, sales, production, and service to boost speed, quality, and margins.

Dive into TransAlta's exemplary journey to SAP Cloud ERP Private | ERP1185

Tuesday, May 12 | 03:00 p.m.

TransAlta Corporation implemented SAP Cloud ERP Private on time and under budget with significant business improvements across finance, supply chain management, HR, project systems, and enterprise asset management. A selective data conversion and clean core/fit-to-standard approach enabled a foundation for AI evaluation and adoption, and growth.

Unlock the future of operations with SAP Cloud ERP Private | ERP1179

Tuesday, May 12 | 04:00 p.m.

Dive deep into innovations empowering the COO— from intelligent supply chain orchestration and predictive asset management to smooth service delivery. Explore how innovative capabilities within your private cloud reduce complexity, boost resilience, and accelerate value.

Explore our next-generation innovations for intelligent asset management | SCM1731

Tuesday, May 12 | 04:30 p.m.

Join us for a live demo highlighting the latest innovations from SAP in planning, scheduling, and mobile execution—and showing where AI can help streamline key steps.

AI and agents in SAP Intelligent Asset Management solutions | SCM1362

Wednesday, May 13 | 10:30 a.m.

Understand how mobile AI, the Field Service Dispatcher Agent, and agents for asset management maintenance planning and failure mode and effects analysis work together to optimize maintenance programs. We look at how you can reduce demand, improve resource allocation and execution, and guide asset investment.

Unveiling the path to autonomous asset and service management | SCM1732

Wednesday, May 13 | 11:00 a.m.

Autonomous Asset and Service Management is moving from vision to reality. In this session we outline how AI will transform maintenance and field service.  ConocoPhillips shares its early co-innovation journey with SAP to move from exploration to concrete business values.

Be future-ready with innovations from SAP Business Network | SCM2142

Wednesday, May 13 | 01:00 p.m.

Explore the future of SAP Business Network and how innovations will reshape collaboration across procurement, supply chain, logistics, and asset management. Learn about AI-assisted capabilities, expanded visibility, and suite-aligned workflows that elevate resilience, intelligence, and connectivity across your trading partner ecosystem.

Running intelligent maintenance with SAP Asset Performance Management | SCM2312

Wednesday, May 13 | 02:00 p.m.

Learn how food manufacturer M. Dias Branco S.A. uses SAP Asset Performance Management and AI to detect anomalies in vibration and process signals, generate alerts and recommendations, and trigger work orders. Discover how this helps improve overall equipment effectiveness and reduces unplanned downtime.

AI-assisted asset and service management solutions | SCM2331

Demo Station | Open During Show Floor Hours

Maximize asset performance and deliver maintenance and service excellence with SAP software. Our solutions help optimize operations through closed-loop orchestration, bringing asset, network, and operational data together in a unified asset thread while unlocking AI-enabled insights and automation.

Start putting together your personal agenda today. Check out the full session catalog and stay updated on all the latest announcements by following us on LinkedIn SAP Supply Chain Management.

entry.title: Re: Compatibility Pack: How to check it in your system going forward?
entry.content:
Hi @Shwethamani_M 

We are currently in a brownfield conversion where SAP NetWeaver 7.5 SP31 is in use, and end users are actively accessing WDJ‑based ESS and MSS applications. As part of the program, the existing ECC 6.0 EHP7 system is being converted to S/4HANA 2023 FPS03. With this target release, Web Dynpro Java–based ESS/MSS applications are not expected to function without remediation.

Could you please advise on the recommended and SAP‑preferred approach to replace WDJ ESS/MSS applications with Web Dynpro ABAP (WDA)–based ESS/MSS applications?

In ECC, the following business packages are typically installed to enable WDA ESS/MSS:

  • HCM_ESS_WDA_1
  • HCM_ESS_WDA_2
  • HCM_MSS_WDA_1
  • HCM_MSS_WDA_2

In the context of S/4HANA with H4S4 activated, I would like to understand:

  • Are WDA‑based ESS/MSS applications delivered as part of the standard H4S4 installation in S/4HANA?
  • Or is the installation and configuration of the same business packages (or their S/4HANA equivalents) still required?

Looking forward to your guidance on the ideal procedure.

Regards,

Bharani

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
@DEEPA_DORAIRAJ  

Great article. I really like how clearly you explained the practical parts of building a RAG pipeline on HANA Cloud. The points on embedding consistency, score thresholds, and re‑embedding cadence are spot on — these are the areas where most teams run into issues when they move from a demo to real production data.

Your explanation of retrieval‑augmented classification also stood out. Using a company’s own historical master data instead of generic industry labels makes a huge difference in accuracy. That’s exactly the kind of real‑world guidance SAP customers need.

One thing I’m curious about as these vector stores grow:
Have you seen any impact on retrieval accuracy or performance when the number of chunks reaches the millions, especially when combining SOURCE_TYPE filtering with cosine similarity?

We’re evaluating whether to keep everything in one unified table or split by domain (KB, DDIC, transports, master data). Any lessons from your production experience would be very helpful.

entry.title: Re: Doubtful Receivables – Provision Method
entry.content: In Public Cloud, provision methods are created via SSCUI in the Manage Your Solution app, not via classic IMG. Search for the activity ‘Define Provision Methods for Doubtful Receivables,’ create the method for company code 2310, and assign it to the valuation area. This will make it available in the Schedule General Ledger Jobs app.

entry.title: Universal Parallel Accounting - Hands on in S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition 2023
entry.content:
UPA is the natural evolution of different SAP ERP functionalities, like for example Costing-Based COPA, Margin Analysis, COGM, Currency and Valuation profiles. UPA brings the goal of having different parallel views  under the lean finance philosophy: simple and efficient processes,  in a single source of truth, easy to consume for business users. 

Activate UPA and unlock management accounting that’s fully integrated into compliance: post different parallel values per ledger across the entire value chain to deliver simultaneous Group and Local GAAP reporting, reduce reconciliation effort, and accelerate complex multi-GAAP implementations.

We are a group of Finance SAP Consultants with different competencies in different fields, we decided to come together to test UPA, following same steps as a new customer would do, putting ourselves in our customer' shoes. We adopted the green field approach, so after obtaining a new system we installed the UPA Business Function and we implemented the SAP UPA best practices.

We would like to concentrate on a "hands on" exercise executed on an S/4HANA 2023 internal system. Please be aware that UPA is available for new Implementation customers from S/4HANA 2022 and from S/4HANA 2023 for Customers having systems with existing data.

BUSINESS CASE 

There are 4 Use cases addressed by Universal Parallel Accounting.

In this article we will concentrate on the first one: Parallel Valuations- Unconsolidated view for International companies which need to report Group and Local GAAP.  

UPA use cases

“Two different ledgers and accounting principles used consistently across the value chain”

(definition by Janet Salmon, chief product owner for SAP Management Accounting).

 

When UPA is active, it is possible to write parallel different values per ledger across the entire value chain. This gives the possibility of being compliant with different accounting principles in the management accounting as well to obtain a complete GAAP financial statement.    

UPA Value Chain

Even  If UPA is not implemented, parallel accounting is still reachable across the value chain but can only copy, in some specific part of the value chain,  the values originally written in the Leading ledger. In these processes the leading ledger value is applied to all ledgers. This may causes need for manual adjustments and/or additional revaluation activities at month-end. 

Value Chain without UPA

The aim of this blog is to analyze each box of the UPA Value Chain in sequence and to demonstrate the results of parallel ledger posting in an unconsolidated view. 

The business scenario described below concerns a company (belonging to a Group of companies) which want to track impact of the different transactional flows (from posting overhead costs  to asset capitalization, depreciation,  to consumption of activities and materials for the production and sales of a finished product), in the Financial statement items (revenues, Investments , Asset Under Constructions, Final Assets, Overheads, Cost of Goods Manufactured, Cost of Goods Sold , Sales Revenues and Margins),  evaluated following different accounting principles. 

 

FUNCTIONAL TESTS

Find below the previous value chain adapted with the functional steps of our functional tests. 

Use Cases Addressed by UPA

In the Best Practices we activated we had Controlling Area Currency Dollar (USD) and Company Code Currency Euro (EUR). So every posting we will have in this functional test  will be in both currencies. Only when entering the cost rate is it managed in a single currency type (the controlling area currency in this example)

So let's start with the first step: Cost posted to the investment internal order.  

1. Posting  a Finance Document on an Internal Order 

Primary cost to Internal Order

 

SCOPE ITEM

J54 Post primary costs on the internal order

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger common manual posting to internal order.

Business Case Coverage

Capitalizable expenses allowed as part of the assets cost, depending on the accounting principle.

For example, financial expenses from a loan related to a short-term construction project: the adjustment may only apply in one ledger (Local GAAP but not IFRS)

With UPA/Without UPA

No differences with installation where UPA is not active. We also decided to use the same values for the 2 ledgers

Please be aware that we decided to use internal orders instead of WBS elements because we wanted to test the internal orders that are still available in the S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition.

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-1

 

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-2

We decided to post the same values in Ledger 0L and 2L (by using ledger group 0L2L). 

F4023 Display Line Items 0L Primary Cost

F4023 Display Line Items 2L Primary Cost

2. Manage Cost Rate Plan

Activity Allocations

We planned here different rates per ledger on both activity types (Setup and Personnel Hours) for brevity we used the same activity types for activity type allocation to an Internal order (next step) and later for Product cost planning calculation. Please note that the controlling area currency used is USD.

F3162 Manage Cost Rates-Plan 0L and 2L

3. Direct Activity Allocation 

Activity Allocations II

SCOPE ITEM

J54 Direct Activity Allocation

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific valuation of activity consumption

Business Case Coverage

Activity allocation (e.g. consulting activities) previously valuated with different prices per ledger are now allocated from the cost center to the Internal Order resulting in different amounts posted by ledger (in order to accommodate local market differences or/and local inventory- asset valuation rules)

With UPA/Without UPA

Activity allocations always post to all ledgers (with or without UPA)

Only with UPA the values posted can differ between ledgers (because of different activity cost rates).

Without UPA, the system uses the ledger-independent cost rate.

See  below the accounting document showing activity allocation from the cost center to the internal order (10 hours per activity type):

F3697 Manage Direct Activity Allocation

Note that there is a single journal entry with postings to two ledgers, based on the reference document 300000300 posted in the previous step.

F4023 Display Line Items 0L

F4023 Display Line Items 2L

As the activity price is 30 USD/hour in ledger 0L and 50 USD/hour in ledger 2L, different amounts are posted per ledger. You can see here that the Activity Allocation From the Cost Center to the internal Order has different values per Ledger.  

4. Settlement- Asset Under Construction  

Settlement AuC

SCOPE ITEM

6DT Asset Under Construction

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger Specific settlement rules  for WBS/ Internal Order according to different accounting principles.

Business Case Coverage

Asset Under Construction capitalization values differs by country based on local GAAP vs. IFRS requirements.

With UPA/Without UPA

UPA allows different settlement rules per ledger.

With UPA, AUC settlement is based on the combination of company code and ledger (FI, CO and AM are ledger-based areas)

Without UPA, settlement values are the same for all ledgers (it´s not an end-to-end  process)

It´s not possible to use UPA without IAA or UPA in connection with “New Asset Accounting”

You can manage different settlement rules per ledger. To simplify this example, the settlement rules in the order are assumed to be identical across all ledgers, as you can see in the image below:

F5695 Manage Setllement Rules-Internal Orders

However, keep in mind that different settlement amounts in the receiver can result from:

  • Ledger-specific settlement rules in the sender object

  • Ledger-specific posted amounts in the sender object

In our case, different settlement results are obtained per ledger due to different posted amounts. 

In the image below, you can see how UPA remains consistent throughout the entire asset lifecycle:

Asset capitalization and depreciation are ledger-specific in UPA, no manual adjustments or clearing are required, ensuring a seamless asset lifecycle (see image below).

Asset Accounting with-without UPA

When UPA is activated, the option for ledger-specific distribution rules is enabled in the configuration activities for the settlement profile.

Chart of depreciation is only used as an object for depreciation key management.

Depreciation areas for parallel currencies are no longer required; each area is automatically managed in all currencies of its assigned ledger.

Capitalization 

Asset Capitalization

 

SCOPE ITEM

J62 Intelligent Asset Accounting

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger Specific settlement rules  for WBS/ order and depreciation run for assets according to different accounting principles.

Business Case Coverage

Asset capitalization and depreciation values differs by country based on local GAAP vs. IFRS requirements.

With UPA/Without UPA

UPA allows different settlement rules per ledger.

With UPA, asset depreciation calculation is based on the combination of company code and ledger (FI, CO and AM are ledger-based areas)

Without UPA, settlement and depreciation values are the same for all ledgers (It´s not an end-to-end  process)

It´s not possible to use UPA without IAA or UPA in connection with “New Asset Accounting”

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 0L

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 2L

As you can see, up to this point we have been working with two currency types: EUR and USD mentioned at the very top of this blog. To simplify the next steps, we will stick to currency type EUR for the Fixed Asset analysis, that results in the following amounts:

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 0L EUR

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 2L EUR

F1684 Manage Fixed Assets

Capitalization (AuC -->Fixed Asset)

The AuC 40000001-0 is settled to Final Fixed Asset 20000000-0.

F3425 Manage Fixed Assets - Final Asset with Reference to AuC 0L

5. Post Depreciation

Asset Depreciation

SCOPE ITEM

J62 Intelligent Asset Accounting

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger Specific settlement rules  for WBS/ order and depreciation run for assets according to different accounting principles.

With UPA/Without UPA

Without UPA, settlement simply takes the values from the leading ledger and copies them into ledger 2L or any other additional ledgers.

This resulted in balances sitting on order/WBS A or order/WBS B that needed to be cleared manually, and they were not representative. The value of the asset under construction in ledger 2L was therefore not reliable at all.

Let’s review the ledger-specific capitalization amounts for assets resulting from internal order settlement to the AuC 40000001-0:

F3425 Manage Fixed Assets- Ledger Specific Postings AuC 0L.

F3425 Manage Fixed Assets- Ledger Specific Postings AuC 2L.

The final asset’s capitalization and depreciation values are ledger-specific:

F3425 Final Asset Capitalization and depreciation ledger specific postings 0L

F3425 Final Asset Capitalization and depreciation ledger specific postings 2L

Once again, the analysis remains available in parallel currencies for both ledgers, as shown in the image below:

F3425 Final Asset Capitalization and depreciation ledger specific postings USD

6. Universal Allocation

Universal Allocation

SCOPE ITEM

J54 Universal Allocation

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger specific Automatic Allocation between Cost Centers

Business Case Coverage

Automatic Allocations of costs per ledger. In this specific case we decided to use the same rules per every ledger .

With UPA/Without UPA

Same result between UPA and S/4HANA (Universal allocation is already able to execute allocation by ledger in S/4HANA). In UPA the only way to distribute cost is the universal allocation. As in S/4HANA Universal Allocation  supports Ledger allocations and 10 currencies.

As said, the Universal allocation functionality is already working per single ledger even without UPA. So to show you how it works we inserted here an exercise but you can execute it in the same way in a not UPA environment. 

In the following picture you can see the allocation flow from cost center 10101101 to 101013012 and 10101301

F4022 Allocation Flow

F4363 Allocation Result Ledger

F4363 Allocation Result 0L Sender

F4363 Allocation Result 0L Receiver

 

F4363 Allocation Result 2L Sender

F4363 Allocation Result 2L Receiver

 7. Standard Cost Calculation

Standard Cost Calculation

 

SCOPE ITEM

BEG Standard Cost Calculation

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Multiple material valuation per ledger and legal view.

Business Case Coverage

Different accounting principles for inventory valuation, depending on local/ global compliance requirements.

With UPA/Without UPA

With UPA multiple cost estimates might be created (in reference to specific ledger costing variant) in order to update the various ledger. The material may be valuated with different inventory price per ledger (a single price can also be used when the cost variant is not assigned to a ledger- ledger field blank). These different prices are available for the full end-to-end flow (with different COGS and contribution margins per ledger)

Without UPA, none of the solutions provided a full E2E process, because they weren’t defined by ledger, but by other parameters.

Parallel accounting enables you to calculate the cost of goods manufactured based on various accounting principles. The underlying accounting principle is assigned to each ledger. By using ledger-specific costing variants, costing types and valuation variants, you can calculate different material prices per ledger. Please check the configuration in your system.

The costing type 2L (linked to the costing variant P02L) is assigned to ledger 2L. It means the costing variant P02L will update only ledger 2L with the results of the material cost estimate, which is expected to be different from Ledger 0L.

The product master data valuation tab contains information about inventory prices across all ledgers and currencies, as shown in the image below:

F1602 Manage Product Master Data Ledgers and Currencies

Likewise, the material stocks are valuated accordingly:

F2680 Manage Material Valuation

After releasing the material cost estimate, inventory prices will be updated per ledger. Stock movements will be valuated differently in each ledger if different prices exist.

It’s not just materials that can have different ledger prices; cost rates for activities in a cost center can vary by ledger too (as seen before). For example, depreciation rates often differ by country, so the the activity "machine hours" can carry different values depending on the ledger. 

Let’s take a look at the following example of direct labor and production setup costs (activity types: 11 and 3):

F3162 Manage Cost Rates-Plan

During a costing run, SAP reads activity cost rates for activity types 3 and 11 according to the ledger assigned to each costing variant. In the example, P00L (ledger 0L) picks up the rates in the red box while P02L (ledger 2L) picks up the rates in the blue box. This setup lets you model country-specific direct labor and production setup costs by ledger automatically, without manual adjustments.

F1865 Manage Costing Runs-P00L

F1865 Manage Costing Runs-P02L

During the release step, the system updates the material prices and creates the corresponding posting documents. The stock revaluation is calculated as:

(new standard price – previous standard price) × stock quantity

resulting in different amounts per ledger and currency type, as shown below.

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-0L

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-2L

8. Product Cost Posting

Production Cost Posting

 

SCOPE ITEM

2UG  3F0 Production Process by ledger event-based

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific WIP and variance calculation or/and optional manual execution.

Business Case Coverage

End-to-end integration between FI and CO with parallel valuation (assets, activities, inventory) enabling multiple accounting principles and extended to CO production processes (WIP and variance settlement)

With UPA/Without UPA

UPA enables valuation of GI/GR and activity consumption at IFRS vs local GAAP rates and also supports ledger-specific settlement of actual production variances.

UPA is only working with the event-based approach (3F0)

Without UPA order settlement (WIP/variances) simply takes the values in the leading ledger and copying them into whatever other additional ledgers.

Let’s go through the production process, starting from the final picture (the calculated actual unit prices) and trace back the path by which those prices are determined for ledger 0L and 2L. This latest calculation is explained further in the blog, in the Actual Costing section.

For simplicity, we will focus on currency type EUR in the Material Price Analysis app, although cross-currency types analysis per ledger is supported.

CKM3N Material Price Analysis- Ledger 0L

CKM3N Material Price Analysis- Ledger 2L

Goods issues (GIs) posted at production order confirmation (using ledger-specific material prices and activity allocations based on each ledger’s cost rates ) may debit different amounts to the order in each ledger:

F3365 Display Document Flow- Order Confirmation GI

You can see the finance postings of material documents 4900000031 and 4900000032 for both ledgers in the image below. Note that, in this case, raw materials do not use different inventory prices per ledger; however, the information is still posted by ledger:

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts debited to the production order 0L

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts debited to the production order 2L

Goods receipts (GR) posted at production order confirmation (using ledger-specific standard material price) may credit different amounts to the order in each ledger.

F3365 Display Document Flow- Order Confirmation GR

You can see the finance postings of material documents 5000000020 for both ledgers in the image below. Note that, in this case, different prices per ledger were used (keep in mind these values come from the released material cost estimate per ledger seen before)

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts credited to the production order 0L

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts credited to the production order 2L.

These values are shown as the preliminary production valuation (GR) in the Material Price Analysis app (see blue box in the attached image below):

CKM3N Preliminary valuation Goods Receipt 0L

CKM3N Preliminary valuation Goods Receipt 2L

9. Event-Based Settlement

Event-Based Settlement

 

SCOPE ITEM

2UG  3F0 Production Process by ledger event-based

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific WIP and variance calculation and settlement.

UPA is only working with the event-based approach.

In the event-based approach (3F0), there are two models to trigger the settlement:

  • Settlement based on business events: Settlement is triggered automatically by business events such as goods movement or activity confirmation (RAK= RSEBW)
  • Manual Settlement: Settlement is initiated manually by setting the Order Settlement Trigger Type to Settled Manually instead of Based on Business Event (recommended for high volume scenarios). 

Please, check the configuration in your system.

During the first partial goods receipt, WIP starts to be reversed out proportionally until the final goods receipt or the CTEC status is reached. At that point, WIP will be completely cleared, and variances will be calculated and assigned to their respective variance categories.

Settlement based on business events provides real-time business insights and reduces period-end closing workload. Manual settlement helps decrease the large volume of settlement documents, especially in scenarios where backflush is heavily used and production orders have short lifecycles, making WIP analysis unnecessary.

Continuing with our example you can see ledger-specific debit and credit postings result in different WIP and variance amounts  calculated for each ledger and posted to the order.

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Variances 0L

The total production price difference for ledger 0L (307,75 EUR) is shown in the CKM3N App:

CKM3N Production Price Difference 0L.

The same logic applies for ledger 2L. In this case, the price difference is 513,01 EUR.

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Variances 2L

CKM3N Production Price Difference 2L

Note that event-based settlement generates one document per triggering event:

F4023 Display Line Items EBVP 0L

10 Preliminary COGS Values

Preliminary COGS Values

Preliminary values for goods issues associated with the sales order are derived from the released standard cost estimate per ledger explained before. Let’s track these types of movements in the Material Price Analysis app:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis 0L

Drilling down into the financial document posted with the goods issue for ledger 0L (two goods issues of 1 unit each)

F4023 Display Line Items 0L Goods Issue

Keep in mind that the COGS splitting document for these preliminary values is also created during the GI:

F4023 Display Line Items 0L Cost Splitting

And for ledger 2L:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis 2L

F4023 Display Line Items 2L Goods Issue

With the correspondent ledger specific COGS splitting:

F4023 Display Line Items 2L Cost Splitting

Verify that released material cost estimates per ledger are applied to stock movements across all currencies and parallel ledgers:

F1602 Manage Product Master Data

11 Actual Costing

Actual Costing

 

SCOPE ITEM

33Q Actual Costing

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific actual material cost

Business Case Coverage

Ledger-specific contribution margin calculation, aligned with different accounting principles, is enabled since actual costing is performed per ledger and COGS are revaluated at the end of the period (PUP). This is an end-to-end process where full margin analysis can be reported by market segment and ledger, as costs are split by ledger across the entire value chain.

With UPA/Without UPA

Monitor Material prices App exclusive for UPA (App Id F7245)

Without UPA actual material inventory prices can only vary based on the valuation view (group/ profit center/ legal), and only one actual inventory price valuation is allowed in the legal view.

Without UPA APP Manage Material Valuations (App Id F2680) should be used instead.

Actual Price calculations are executed on a per-ledger basis: the Material Ledger derives actual prices and variance allocations separately for each ledger, so each ledger receives its own calculated actual price for materials. As a result, period-end ML close postings (inventory revaluation and COGS revaluation) are computed and posted per ledger, yielding different inventory values and cost of goods sold (COGS) amounts by ledger.

This closes the parallel valuation loop, enabling parallel FI postings and discrete contribution-margin reporting in Margin Analysis. In short, ledger-specific UPA plus Material Ledger close support a parallel value flow across end‑to‑end processes and let you analyse margins by ledger (see section 12. Profit Margins)

The different actual prices per ledger that are calculated for the legal valuation view can be analyzed and compared:

F7245 Monitor Material Prices- Actual Prices

Or analysed for all valuation types, where you can see differences between actual prices, inventory prices and standard prices (for example you can see that the actual price for ledger 0L is 25,46 EUR, while the standard price is 22,81 EUR)

F7245 Monitor Material Prices

Stock and GI (COGS) are updated by ledger with the corresponding adjustment and market segments assignments (product, sales order...) when actual costing run is performed, adjusting preliminary COGS values (See section 8. Preliminary COGS Values) and posting both the actual values for COGS and the ending inventory values (delta posting). Let’s take a look at the results for Ledger 0L:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis-Actual Costing 0L

Line-Items posted during actual costing run:

CKMLCP Actual Costing Run 0L

The adjustment for ending inventory is calculated as follows:

(actual price - standard price) * ending inventory quantity = (25,46€/Kg- 22,81 €/kg) * 198 Kg = 524,45 €

The adjustment for COGS is calculated as follows: 

(actual price - standard price) *GI from sales order quantity (period) = (25,46€/Kg- 22,81 €/kg) * 2Kg = 5,3 €

Let’s take a look at the results for Ledger 2L:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis-Actual Costing 2L

Line-Items posted during actual costing run:

CKMLCP Actual Costing Run 2L

12 Profit Margin

Profit Margin

To complete the E2E flow, let’s review how margins are affected depending on the ledger/accounting principle used for their calculation:

F0960 Market Segments- Actual Ledger 0L

Margin of 69.08 EUR: 120 EUR revenues minus 50.92 EUR Actual COGS (2uds * 25.46 EUR).

Actual COGS are calculated based on actual material costs. Note that the COGS split is retrieved from the actual costing cost components for ledger 0L  and a delta posting is created between actual and standard cost component splits from the preliminary COGS values (see Section 8. Preliminary COGS Values )

You can check it in the image below where the quantity is set to 2, which is the same as the sales quantity:

CKM3N Cost Components 0L

That comes from PUP calculated during actual costing run: 25,46 €/UN

CKM3N- Material Price Analysis Ledger 0L

Same logic applies to ledger 2L, with every specific ledger amounts:

F0960 Market Segments- Actual Ledger 2L

Margin of 51,92 EUR: 120 EUR revenues minus 68.08 EUR Actual COGS (2uds * 34.04 EUR).

CKM3N Cost Components 2L

CKM3N- Material Price Analysis Ledger 2L

Other App to analyze ledger-specific contribution margins:

W0182 Product profitability with production variances ledgers 0L and 2L

This brings us to the final step of the end-to-end process example.

Another Important part of the UPA implementation is the Best Practice Activation  

BEST PRACTICES ACTIVATION (AND DEACTIVATION) 

As you may know,  you can find SAP Best Practices in the SAP SIGNAVIO Process Navigator.

When Activating SAP Best Practices to Implement UPA, the first thing to be considered from a functional point of view,  is which UPA Best Practices needs to be activated and which ones (not valid for UPA) needs to be deactivated deactivated. Second thing to consider is required business functions. Please pay extra attention to SAP's standard documentation based on your system version. In fact UPA has its own Best Practices and request to deactivate some others. Furthermore, UPA has it's own post activation steps based on business scenarios you're going execute on system. You can check further details in the  Administration Guide

Please keep in mind that UPA comes with a set of UX mandates like usage of new Fiori apps instead of GUI transaction codes which makes Fiori usage a must for the system.

When setting up a Best Practice Client, specially in a multiple country activation,  one important step is to set up wisely the client currency (transaction SCC4). Also, how to setup that client also makes activation step more complicated or simple. We have chosen the "Alternative 1" to make it more simple. "Alternative 2" brings some pre-delivered configuration content from client "000" and some of them may be conflicting the ones delivered with Best Practice content.  Please check the Administration Guide for further details.

Some specific UPA best practices are related to the activation of the FINS_PARALLEL_ACCOUNTING_BF  business function. Please check the Administration Guide.

P.S. Regardless of UPA activation, please pay extra attention on activating required business functions and selecting best practices since there are some licensing implications.

To conclude, there are already many other blog posts written by the Product Owner and other SAP community members .

Universal Parallel Accounting in S/4HANA 

Overhead Accounting with Universal Parallel Accounting in S/4HANA 2025

Overhead Accounting with Universal Parallel Accounting S/4HANA 2022

Profit Center Valuation with Universal Parallel Accounting S/4HANA 2023

Inventory Accounting with Universal Parallel Accounting in S/4HANA 2022 

Please be aware that as stated by OSS 3191636 UPA can be activated via a Business Function. For details, please refer to the  Business Function Documentation

If you have more questions about UPA you can check the UPA FAQ contained in OSS  3265275;

If you want to start your journey with UPA it is suggested to apply for SAP Early adopter care program in UPA   

If you need to know how functionalities may evolve in future S/4HANA releases please have a look to the SAP Roadmap Explorer

Brought to you by a group of SAP Consultants in collaboration with the S/4HANA RIG 

Helena Carro @Helenacarro 

Mehmet Canca @mehmetcanca 

Vineek Gopan @Vineek 

Gianluca Taccone @gianluca_taccone 

Javier Trinidad  @FJTM

entry.title: Re: Mass update of Control indicators for MICs which are...
entry.content:
Dear @Arvinder_Rauthan1,

In Public Cloud this requirement is not covered by a standard mass-maintenance function in the Master Recipe apps. Standard recipe maintenance is done through the Master Recipe apps at individual recipe/operation/phase level. SAP documents the use of control keys and inspection characteristics at master recipe operation/phase level but does not provide a standard mass-change action for MIC control indicators assigned to recipe phases. SAP MIC documentation also makes the important distinction between reference characteristics and complete copy models. Therefore, the correction approach depends on how the MIC was assigned and whether the relevant values are still controlled at MIC level or were copied/maintained in the recipe phase

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing-execution/configuring-manufacturing-master-data

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/bcbbbb53707db44ce10000000a174cb4.html

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F5426')/S36

The clean standard workaround depends on how the MICs were assigned:

  1. If the required change can be controlled at MIC master data level, MIC change/versioning should be considered first, depending on how the MIC was assigned and used
  2. If the MICs were copied into the recipe phase, there is no standard mass update to push changed control indicators into all existing recipes. The affected recipes/phases need to be corrected manually or through a controlled API-based approach provided the required fields are exposed and updateable in the released API metadata. SAP documents Master Recipe API operations such as Update Master Recipe Phase and inspection-characteristic update operations but this must be validated field-by-field before confirming automation

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d35113ee62644d3abee1aaec148291d9/691128d324c74be085d401ac284455ba.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d35113ee62644d3abee1aaec148291d9/81b2298304b44425a2fbcc009d83588b.html

Summary:

There is no standard Public Cloud mass-change app for updating MIC control indicators already assigned to Master Recipe phases. The clean options are MIC-level change/versioning where applicable or manual/API-based correction after validating that the required released API fields are exposed and updateable

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Applying 'Techncial User Propagation' to API Management
entry.content:
This is either generated in as a keypair in your BTP Subaccount -> Destinations Certificate and retrieved via Platform API calls as a P12/PFX file and loaded into the Keystore of APIM or it can be generated and maintained separately (but requires it to be loaded as a Keystore in APIM to use).

One note if you decide to create a Destinations Certificate keypair as we just ran into this:
If you set the auto-renewal function to true, when it auto-renews after your set time, you MUST remove the Keystore entry in APIM and create it anew each time the certificate updates it's new validity date.

There may be a way to utilize a Destination to support the auto-renewal completely; however, this process to update the Keystore each time is a bit cumbersome as there is no reminder that the Destination Certificate entry is auto-renewing and there is action required on APIM.

entry.title: Strategic Data Paradigms for building AI-ready IT Systems
entry.content:
Data Fabric, Data Mesh, and Knowledge Graphs are key strategic data paradigms for data professionals to build their data and AI architectures. Several experts have shared their knowledge and expertise on these topics in SAP community channels. The purpose of this article is to offer a high-level summary and share a few important articles for data professionals seeking to learn more about what SAP offers or become familiar with this topic. I recommend bookmarking this article, as I plan to update it with the latest information as it becomes available. 

True AI innovations can only happen when agentic AI applications have access to the most accurate data. Unlocking the full potential of AI across the enterprise requires a simplified architecture strategy to optimize data silos, improve data integrity, and build user trust.  

Data fabric is a combination of data architecture and dedicated solutions that enables you to connect to and manage all your data, including transactional and analytical data, in real time across different systems and applications.  

Business data fabric, the future of data and AI, enhances data fabric architecture by retaining business logic and context within the data. It helps stakeholders gain swift, trusted access to comprehensive data. For example, when SAP data is extracted and copied across multiple systems, it loses business context. And enterprises spend significant time and effort to rebuild it for analytics. A business data fabric eliminates the need for data duplication and rebuilding of business context. 

Data mesh is a data management methodology that uses a distributed architecture framework. It distributes ownership and responsibility for specific datasets across the business users with the specialist experience to understand what that data means and how to make the best use of it.

Knowledge Graph connects complex relationships within data. It provides context and shows how entities are related, what matters most, and which patterns are meaningful. This grounding plays a critical role in ensuring AI models provide accurate, trustworthy results while significantly reducing the likelihood of hallucinations. 

Putting these concepts together, SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) serves as SAP’s business data fabric, connecting, modeling, and governing SAP and non-SAP data while preserving business meaning from source to consumption. It supports the data mesh operating model to meet organizational needs with spaces and data products where a team or LOB manages its own data, models, pipelines, and federated governance with role-based access controls. With a knowledge graph as a built-in feature, it will serve as a semantic backbone that connects business data and metadata across systems, so users and AI can navigate it by meaning rather than by tables and joins. Finally, intelligent applications in SAP BDC automate, adapt, and learn in real time, powered by AI that understands your business. 

The scale of data volumes and LOB data governance requirements help you define a data mesh methodology using SAP’s business data fabric to build a robust data and AI platform for your business. 

Below is a good collection of articles and blog posts from experts worth reading to learn more. 

 

Data Fabric: 

  • What is a data fabric? 
  • What is a Business Data Fabric? 
  • Blog: A Business Data Fabric for Data and AI Science 
  • Blog: SAP Business Data Fabric: Unifying Your Enterprise Data with BDC 
  • Blog: Modernize Your SAP Business Warehouse Environment with a Cloud-Based Data Fabric 
  • Blog: 5 Steps to a Business Data Fabric 
  • Blog: How to Learn SAP Datasphere – The Cloud-Based Data Warehouse / Data Fabric Solution 

Data Mesh: 

  • Article: What is data mesh? 
  • Blog: From Vision to Reality: Enabling Data Mesh with SAP Business Data Cloud 
  • Data Mesh Architecture with SAP Datasphere (several references in this article) 
  • Data Mesh with SAP Business Technology Platform, BTP: efficiency, insights and innovation 

Data Fabric & Data Mesh: 

  • Best Practice Guide: Data fabric vs. data mesh 

 Knowledge Graph: 

  • What is a knowledge graph? 
  • Blog: Knowledge Graphs on Datasphere and HANA Cloud. The differences 
  • Blog: Converting Property Graphs to Knowledge Graphs in SAP HANA Cloud: A Simple Guide
  • Blog: Knowledge Graphs - Datasphere vs HANA Cloud

SAP Business Data Cloud: 

  • SAP Business Data Cloud 
  • SAP Learning: Introducing SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Trial: Service Definition and Binding Not Working newly Created Package
entry.content:
Hi SAP RAP Champion,

my package under ZLOCAL 



I attempted to create a Service Definition and Service Binding in the package ZFE_ATS_PR. However, when I preview the service binding, it displays a blank screen.

 

I created a similar Service Definition and Service Binding using the same CDS root entity (without creating a new CDS root) in another package, ZFE_TRAVEL_000405, which was generated by the class ZDMO_CL_FE_TRAVEL_GENERATOR(https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/fiori-tools-rap-prepare-service..html), and it works as expected.

Additionally, when I run the newly created artifacts (Service Definition → Service Binding) in the package created by the autogenerated class, they work correctly. I also tried previewing the service binding from another user’s package, and it works fine there as well.

To troubleshoot further, I deleted my ABAP Cloud Trial cockpit account and recreated it, but the issue still persists. am missing any configuration, authorization, or package-related setting?

Your valuable suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks,
Pradhan

entry.title: Build Solutions for SAP Cloud ERP with confidence
entry.content:
Partner Reference Application Family provides Partners with a “golden path” to becoming a SaaS provider of multi-tenant applications based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). The guidance covers building, running, and integrating scalable full-stack cloud applications. Whether you are building a brand-new SaaS product or upgrading an existing one, these resources act as a blueprint for success.

It provides ready-to-run code branches and tutorials to accelerate the development of scalable SaaS solutions. It consists of multiple public SAP-samples repositories on GitHub, offering comprehensive guidance aligned with the “principle of one” and “clean core”. These resources highlight the value of the extensibility environment, demonstrate best practices in code quality and architecture, and serve as demo-ready examples for realistic partner extensions.

CAP Partner Reference Application
A GitHub repository featuring the sample multi-tenant solution “Poetry Slam Manager” built on SAP BTP CAP, along with detailed tutorials for developers and application managers.

When delivering standardized SaaS solutions to multiple customers, customer-specific requirements are inevitable. Using SAP Build and CAP, the solution is extensible out of the box, enabling easy customization. The companion repository, "Partner Reference Application Extension", provides guidance on building and deploying such extensions, including a sample customer-specific extension (“Catering Management”).

ABAP Partner Reference Application

For building ABAP-based side-by-side multi-tenant applications for SAP Cloud ERP, refer to the end-to-end guide. It includes the sample solution “Music Festival Manager” based on ABAP Cloud.

Cross Stack Partner Reference Application
Designed for scenarios requiring on-stack extensions on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with seamless integration to SAP BTP CAP applications.
This example demonstrates how an on-stack extension integrates with a side-by-side CAP multi-tenant application (Poetry Slam Manager). It includes tutorials to help build and integrate both components, enabling enterprise-grade cross-stack extensions using ABAP Cloud and CAP.

On-Stack Partner Reference Application
Focused on delivering on-stack extensions for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with scalability, upgrade stability, and seamless integration.
This application integrates with Manage Sales Orders – Version 2 (SAP Fiori app) and showcases event-driven loyalty program management. It demonstrates how standard SAP business processes can be enhanced without modifying the core. Top of Form

 

The Partner Reference Application Family is your accelerator for building next-generation SAP solutions. It’s not just about code—it’s about enabling partners to deliver scalable, extensible, and future-proof applications with speed and quality. By leveraging these ready-made blueprints and best practices, you can reduce development effort, ensure clean core compliance, and focus on what truly matters: delivering value to your customers.

entry.title: Pre Hook External Hook calling V2 REST API returns "Connection reset by peer" - SAP Sales Cloud V2
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am implementing a duplicate CNPJ validation using an External Pre Hook
on the Account entity in SAP Sales Cloud V2. The hook is built in Java
(Spring Boot) deployed on BTP Cloud Foundry.

SCENARIO:
When a user saves an Account with an identification of type Z0001 (CNPJ),
my Pre Hook fires and should check if any other account already has the
same CNPJ. If a duplicate is found, the save should be blocked.

PROBLEM:
When the Pre Hook fires and my service tries to call V2's REST API to
check for duplicates, V2 resets the connection:

ERROR: recvAddress(..) failed: Connection reset by peer
Request to GET https://<tenant>.crm.cloud.sap/sap/c4c/api/v1/
account-service/accounts?filter=identifications/identificationId
eq 'X'&top=2

Because the connection is reset, my catch block fires and returns
noChanges:true (fail-open), allowing the duplicate CNPJ to be saved.

HOOK CONFIGURATION:
Object: Account Service → Account
Event: Pre Hook
Method: POST
Auth: Basic Auth (Communication System)

MY QUESTION:
Is it expected that V2's REST API cannot be called from within a
Pre Hook on the same entity? Is the transaction lock preventing
inbound API calls during hook execution?

I found this blog post that uses a BTP Destination with CAP + OAuth
to call V2 from within a hook:
https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/
validation-using-external-hooks-in-sap-sales-cloud-v2/ba-p/14120109

Is using a BTP Destination with OAuth (instead of Basic Auth) the
correct way to call V2 from within a Pre Hook? Or is there another
recommended approach?

WHAT I ALREADY TRIED:
1. Direct WebClient call with Basic Auth → Connection reset by peer
2. Increasing timeout to 8 seconds → same result
3. The exact same API call works perfectly OUTSIDE the hook context

RESPONSE FORMAT:
My service correctly returns the following when a duplicate is found:
{
"noChanges": true,
"error": [{
"code": "DUPLICATE_CNPJ",
"message": "CNPJ already registered on account: X",
"target": "identifications"
}]
}
Content-Type: application/json;charset=UTF-8

But because the V2 API call fails first, the catch block returns
noChanges:true before the error response is ever built.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Working with DMS in SAP Build Process Automation — A Complete API Guide
entry.content:
Introduction: In one of my recent projects, I had a scenario where I needed to handle Excel files to create or modify mass cost centers. For this, I definitely needed a way to manage files — so I used DMS (Document Management Service)to handle the file operations. 

DMS (Document Management Service) is a key service in SAP BTP for managing files in the cloud. When working with SAP Build Process Automation (BPA), DMS serves as the file storage — when a user uploads a file through a Form Trigger, it is stored in DMS.

Note: Upload attachments to forms, you need an active SAP Document Management Service subscription. With an active subscription, you must then configure your attachment destinations. Before an attachment destination is configured, the attachment option isn't visible when editing a form (Configure SAP Document Management Service for Process Attachments | SAP Help Portal).

More importantly, you need to know how to download files from DMS. Consider a scenario where you need to validate an uploaded file — say, perform Excel-based validations while the file is on the local desktop. After all processing is complete, you can delete the file from DMS. This is the right approach for maintaining storage hygiene in certain scenarios (not all).

You should also understand how to upload files to DMS. Before moving into BPA development, it is essential to test the DMS APIs independently using Bruno (or any other API testing tool like Postman). This blog walks you through exactly that.

Prerequisites

  1. SAP BTP Subaccount with DMS entitlement enabled
  2. DMS Service Instance & Service Key created
  3. Bruno (or any API testing tool) installed
  4. DMS Destination configured in SAP BTP
    • To create a destination from scratch, refer to the official documentation:
      Create Destinations from Scratch – SAP Help
    • After creating the destination, it will appear under Control Tower → Destinations
    • Use a testing tool to verify the connection is working before integrating with BPA

Note: Before connecting BPA directly to the destination, always validate the APIs independently using Bruno.

Step 1: Authentication – Get Access Token 

To perform any CRUD operation on DMS, you need a Bearer Token for authentication. Here's how to obtain it in Bruno:

  1. Open the Auth tab
  2. Select OAuth 2.0 from the dropdown
  3. Set Grant Type to Client Credentials
  4. Fill in the following fields:

Name Value

Access Token URL

<token-url>/oauth/token

Client ID

<your-client-id>

Client Secret

<your-client-secret>

  1. Click Get Access Token — the token will appear under the Grant Type section

Additional Setup:

  • API Key: If required, add it under the Headers tab
  • Proxy: You must configure a proxy for authentication; otherwise, the request will fail

Step 2: Download a Document

To download a document from DMS, use the following:

Method: GET
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Query Parameters:

Name Value

objectId

<file-or-folder-id>

cmisselector

properties

filter

cmis:name

BPA Script – Get File Properties:

let options = {
    method:'GET',
    resolveBodyOnly:true,
    responseType:'json',
    timeout:59000
}
if(fileId){
options.url="/root?objectId=" + fileId.split('folderid:')[1];
}
return options;

 

 

BPA Script – Retrieve File Name:

//retriving file name 
let filename =folderId.objects[0].object.properties["cmis:name"].value;
return filename;

BPA Script – Generate Download URL and Headers:

//Generating the URL and Headers Payload
return{
    url:destination.destinationConfiguration.url+ '/root?objectId='+fileId+'&cmisSelector=content',
    options:{
        headers:{
            Authorization: 'Bearer ' + destination.authTokens[0].value
        }
    }
};

 

Step 3: Get Folder Details 

Method: GET
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Query Parameters:

Type Name Value

Param

objectId

<folder-object-id>

Param

cmisselector

properties

Param

filter

cmis:objectTypeId

Param

filter

cmis:name

Auth: Pass your access token under Auth tab → Bearer Token

Note: you have to give proxy to for authentication otherwise it will not work 

BPA Script - To get folder details

let options = {
    method:'GET',
    url:'/root?objectId='+objectId.split('folderid:')[1]+'&cmisselector=properties',
    responseType:'json',
    timeout:59000
}
return options;

Step 4: Upload a Document

Use this when you need to upload a file to DMS via BPA — for example, when receiving a file from a different source.

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Multipart Form):

Key Value

file

<select your file>

propertyId[0]

cmis:objectTypeId

propertyValue[0]

cmis:document

propertyId[1]

cmis:name

propertyValue[1]

<file-name>

Query Parameters:

Name Value

cmisaction

createDocument

BPA Script - to Upload File

 var options = {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/root?cmisaction=createDocument', 
        responseType: 'json',
        resolveBodyOnly: true,
        metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
        metadata: [
            { name: 'propertyId[0]', value: 'cmis:name' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[0]', value: fileName },
            { name: 'propertyId[1]', value: 'cmis:objectTypeId' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[1]', value: 'cmis:document' },
            { name: 'media', file: filePath }
        ]
};
return options;

Step 5: Create Folder

To create a folder within DM,  use the following

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Form Data):

Name Value

cmisaction

createFolder

propertyId[0]

cmis:objectTypeId

propertyValue[0]

cmis:folder

propertyId[1]

cmis:name

propertyValue[1]

<folder-name>

BPA Script

 var options = {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/root?',
        responseType: 'json',
        resolveBodyOnly: true,
        metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
        metadata: [
            {name:'cmisaction', value: 'createFolder'},
            { name: 'propertyId[0]', value: 'cmis:objectTypeId' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[0]', value: 'cmis:folder' },
            { name: 'propertyId[1]', value: 'cmis:name' },
            { name: 'propertyValue[1]', value: folderName },
        ]
};
return options;

Step 6: Delete a Document/Folder

To delete a document or folder tree from DMS, use the following:

Method: POST
URL: <base-url>/browser/<repository-id>/root

Body (Form Data):

Name Value

cmisaction

deleteTree

objectId

<file-or-folder-id>

unfileObjects

delete

continueOnFailure

true

 

BPA Script - To delete

let objectId = folderId.split('folderid:')[1];
let options = {
    method: 'POST',
    resolveBodyOnly: true,
    responseType: 'json',
    metadataType: irpa_core.enums.request.metadataType.formData,
    form:{
        cmisaction:'deleteTree',
        objectId: objectId,
        unfileObjects:'delete',
        continueOnFailure:true
    }
};
return options;

 

Conclusion :

And that's a wrap! By now, you should have a solid understanding of how to work with DMS in the context of SAP Build Process Automation. We covered the full lifecycle — downloading files to your local machine for validation, getting folder details to inspect what's stored, uploading documents from external sources, creating folders to keep things organized, and deleting files once they've served their purpose.

The key takeaway? Always test your DMS APIs  before wiring them into BPA. It's a small step that saves hours of debugging later. Once you're confident the APIs work as expected, integrating them into your automation becomes straightforward.

I hope this guide helps you build more robust and well-tested BPA workflows. If you have questions or run into issues, feel free to drop a comment — happy to help!.

entry.title: NLQ in the real world: what metadata setup made it successful for you?
entry.content:
I recently shared a demo of natural language query (NLQ) and conversational analytics showing the just ask feature in SAP Analytics Cloud and how Joule takes NLQ further into a conversational experience. We also highlighted before that success depends heavily on metadata, including labels for measures, dimensions, and attributes you plan to use. As SAP continues investing in NLQ and conversational analytics in SAP Business Data Cloud, we’d love to learn from your experience.

1) What’s your current status for NLQ (just ask) or conversational analytics (analytical insights in Joule)?

  • Works reliably
  • Works sometimes
  • Not working / not adopted yet
  • Haven’t tried yet

2) What model type are you using?

The just ask feature and so analytical insights in Joule support acquired models in SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere models.

3) What was the single most important metadata edit that improved results?

Examples:

  • Adding/cleaning business-friendly labels for measures/dimensions/attributes
  • Translating labels for multilingual usage
  • Switching to (or refining) a model structure that’s better suited

4) What’s the most common challenge you face?

Examples:

  • Wrong measure/dimension matched
  • Terms not understood
  • Ambiguous business language (“sales”, “revenue”, “margin”, etc.)
  • Time logic confusion (quarters, fiscal periods, etc.)
  • The model feels “too technical” for business users

5) If you’re willing, share one example:

  • The question you asked: e.g., “Show me actuals vs forecast by region.”
  • What you expected:
  • What you got instead:
  • What metadata change fixed it (or didn’t):

Thanks in advance, if we get enough examples, I’ll summarize patterns back to the community (common challenges + top wins) and turn the best practices into a short checklist focused on metadata readiness for NLQ and conversational analytics.

entry.title: Re: NLQ in the real world: what metadata setup made it successful for you?
entry.content:
Your turn!  Here’s a quick template to facilitate sharing your experience. Feel free to answer only the parts you can.

1) Status today

  • Overall experience: reliable / sometimes / not working / haven’t tried
  • Primary users: business analysts / power users / executives / mixed

2) Use case

  • Feature: just ask or analytical insights in Joule (or both)
  • Model type: SAC acquired model / SAP Datasphere model (or other)

3) The single most impactful metadata change:

  • Better labels for measures/dimensions/attributes
  • Adding business-friendly synonyms / terminology alignment
  • Translating labels into the NLQ language we use
  • Cleaning up ambiguous terms (e.g., “sales” vs “revenue”)
  • Other

4) What remains a challenge:

  • Wrong measure/dimension matched
  • Terms not understood / language mismatch
  • Ambiguity (“margin”, “profit”, “net sales”, etc.)
  • Time logic confusion (“Q2”, fiscal periods, YTD)
  • Model is too technical / not business-term friendly
  • Other

5) One example:

  • Question we asked:
  • The answer we expected:
  • What we got instead:
  • What we changed in metadata to improve it:

6) Bonus: if someone asked me “what’s the #1 thing to do first to make NLQ/conversational analytics work,” I’d say: ...

entry.title: How We Built JUNO Joule Agent for Release Testing Automation Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

From my experience working on multiple SAP SuccessFactors release cycles, one thing became very clear early on — automation alone is not enough. What really makes a difference is how intelligently we manage and execute that automation.

In most projects, teams already invest a lot of effort in building automated test scenarios using internal frameworks or QA tools. But the real challenge starts during release time — deciding what to run, when to run it, and how to interpret the results.

I’ve personally seen teams struggle with simple but critical things like:

  • Manually triggering test executions
  • Switching between tools to track execution status
  • Trying to understand raw test results
  • Mapping technical failures back to business impact

Even though automation exists, the process still feels manual and fragmented.

This gap is exactly what led us to build JUNO Joule Agent — to make release testing not just automated, but also intelligent and easy to manage.    

Current Challenge

In most SAP SuccessFactors implementations I’ve worked on, organizations already have:

  • Automated test scenarios built in their release testing frameworks
  • CI/CD pipelines capable of triggering executions

But what’s usually missing is:

  • A simple, business-friendly way to trigger tests
  • Smart selection of relevant test scenarios per release
  • Clear visibility into execution results
  • A guided or conversational way to validate releases

Because of this, even though testing is technically automated, the overall process still depends heavily on manual effort.

Our Approach – JUNO Joule Agent

To solve this problem, we designed JUNO Joule Agent, an AI-powered release testing solution built on SAP Business Technology Platform.

While working on this, my main focus was to ensure the solution feels natural within the SAP ecosystem — secure, scalable, and easy for both technical and business users.

As part of the implementation, we built the solution using SAP Build Process Automation, leveraging Joule Actions, Skills, and Agent capabilities to orchestrate the entire release testing flow. This helped us connect user requests directly with backend automation in a seamless way.

Instead of creating new test scripts again, JUNO reuses existing automated test scenarios from the release testing framework and brings everything into one place.

With this approach, teams can trigger test execution using simple natural language, monitor execution in real time, and analyze results without switching across multiple tools.

It basically adds an intelligent layer on top of existing automation, which is what was missing earlier.

Benefits

1. Centralized Test Dashboard

  • Displays a unified dashboard to execute and monitor test scenarios, along with options to export results.
  • Provides visibility into test scenarios and execution status across different modules.

 

 Figure 1: JUNO Joule Agent showing list of configured Joule Skills, Agents and release testing artifacts

 

2. Intelligent Test Execution via Joule

  • Accepts natural language user requests and identifies the corresponding release test scenario.

  • Triggers test execution directly from the conversation and provides execution summary and monitoring details.

 

Figure 2:  Natural Language-Based Test Execution using Joule with real-time execution summary and monitoring details

 

Figure 3: Real-time test result insights using Joule showing passed and failed scenario counts

 

3. Faster and More Efficient Testing

  • From what I observed during implementation, this significantly reduced repetitive manual testing effort and helped teams complete release cycles faster with fewer errors.

  • It also allowed HR and IT teams to focus more on analysis and improvements instead of spending time on manual execution.

Figure 4: Triggering multiple release test scenarios using Joule with execution status and monitoring details

 

4. Release Highlights Available in the UI

  • Displays SAP SuccessFactors release updates directly within the application.

  • Shows key changes and related information in a structured format for quick reference.

 5. Testing Across SAP SuccessFactors HCM Modules

  • Supports release testing across multiple SAP SuccessFactors modules, including Employee Central, Onboarding, and Recruiting.

  • Retrieves available test scenarios across these modules using Joule.

 

Figure 5:  Conversational retrieval of Employee Central data using Joule with real-time response display

 

Figure 6: Detailed view of Time Off test cases and execution insights retrieved through Joule conversational interface

 

Figure 7: Conversational view of release RCM test cases and associated scenario details using Joule

6. Test Scenario Execution Using Inputs

  • Allows users to run test scenarios for a specific Entity ID instead of executing all scenarios.

  • Executes only the relevant test cases based on the provided input.

 

Figure 8: Input-driven hierarchical test scenario execution using Joule with detailed execution summary

 

Figure 9: Triggering release test scenarios using Joule with execution success status and details

 7. Release Test Coverage Across SAP SuccessFactors Modules

  • Displays the number of automated test scenarios available for each SAP SuccessFactors HCM module.
  • Retrieves test scenario counts across modules using Joule.

 

Figure 10: Conversational retrieval of test scenario counts across all SAP SuccessFactors modules using Joule

 

Figure 11: Real-time insight into average execution time of release test scenarios using Joule

 

Business Value

From my hands-on experience, JUNO brings clear improvements in how release testing is handled:

- Makes the release testing process more structured and easier to manage across different modules
- Significantly reduces manual effort by enabling test execution through natural language and centralized monitoring
- Ensures consistent and repeatable testing, helping avoid missed validations during release cycles
- Minimizes the risk of critical issues by improving test coverage and visibility
- Improves collaboration between HR and IT teams through clear and accessible execution insights
- Runs securely on SAP Business Technology Platform and scales effectively across multiple modules
- In my experience, this also improved overall confidence during release cycles, as teams had better visibility and control over test execution

Conclusion

Release testing in SAP SuccessFactors is critical, but managing it can become complex with frequent updates and multiple dependencies.

From what I’ve seen in real projects, adding an intelligent layer like JUNO Joule Agent completely changes the way teams approach testing. Instead of worrying about tools, scripts, or execution steps, teams can directly focus on validating business scenarios.

For me, the biggest impact was the significant reduction in manual effort, improved clarity across the testing process, and overall smoother and faster release cycles.

This approach not only improves efficiency but also changes how teams think about release testing—from a technical task to a more business-focused validation process.

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
@Swetha__Balusamy 

Good question — they are different approaches. The Document Grounding service in SAP AI Core is SAP's managed grounding layer that handles document ingestion, chunking, embedding, and retrieval through a configured pipeline without you building the vector store infrastructure yourself. What this article describes is building the RAG pipeline directly on HANA Cloud Vector Store — you manage the embeddings, the table structure, the retrieval logic, and the threshold tuning yourself via Python and the Generative AI Hub SDK.

Both achieve grounding but at different levels of control. Document Grounding is faster to set up and SAP-managed. The HANA Cloud approach gives you full control over chunking strategy, embedding model selection, score thresholds, and re-embedding cadence — which is why this article goes that route for production use cases where those parameters matter. If you're just getting started, Document Grounding is the lower-friction path. If you need production-grade control, build directly on HANA Cloud Vector Store.

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
@Atul_Joshi85 

Thank you — really glad the production-focused sections landed well. Your question on scale is one of the most important architectural decisions in enterprise RAG and there isn't a universal answer, but here's what I've seen work:

On retrieval accuracy at millions of chunks — cosine similarity with SOURCE_TYPE filtering holds well at scale if your embeddings are consistent (same model, same chunking strategy throughout). Where accuracy degrades is usually not the vector search itself but embedding drift — chunks embedded at different times with different models or different preprocessing. The re-embedding cadence section in the article exists precisely because this is where most production systems quietly degrade.

On unified table vs domain split — I'd recommend splitting by domain, and here's the practical reason: SOURCE_TYPE filtering on a unified table works at moderate scale but becomes a bottleneck when you're combining it with cosine similarity across millions of rows. HANA Cloud's vector engine handles within-partition searches more efficiently than cross-partition filtered searches. Splitting by domain (KB, DDIC, transports, master data) gives you natural partitioning, cleaner re-embedding management per domain, and the ability to tune score thresholds independently — master data typically needs a higher threshold than transport history, for example.

The one case where unified makes sense is when your queries are genuinely cross-domain — if a single user query should retrieve from KB and master data simultaneously. In that case, parallel queries to domain-split tables with result merging at the application layer gives you the best of both.
